data_availability_interpretation.interpretation: The V5 discovery found OI, liquidation, and funding coverage across all 10 probe symbols with 15min as the best usable interval, supporting recent-data mechanism studies but not long-history OHLCV-style lockbox claims.
data_availability_interpretation.recommended_scope: Recent Coinalyze-only dataset build followed by event studies before any strategy backtest.

anti_overfit_requirements: No parameter grid in the first event study.
Use round structural thresholds only, such as top decile event buckets or fixed multiples of recent median liquidation volume.
Chronologically split recent data if enough history exists; do not use later segments to tune earlier definitions.
Compare event windows to simple time-block nulls before any backtest is considered.
No candidate, edge, live, or capital permission from event-study results.
hypothesis_designs.expected_direction: Upward mean reversion after the liquidation cascade completes and OI has flushed.
hypothesis_designs.mechanism: After a strong sell move, a large long-liquidation spike with falling open interest can mark forced selling exhaustion rather than voluntary risk transfer.
hypothesis_designs.minimum_history_needed: At least 30 recent 15m days for a first event study; 60 or more days preferred for chronological segmentation.
no_trade_conditions: Liquidation spike occurs without OI contraction.
Funding and long/short ratio still indicate fresh long crowding after the event.
Broad market remains in an accelerating sell cascade.
Event occurs in illiquid or incomplete data windows.
overfitting_risks: Choosing best liquidation-spike threshold after seeing outcomes.
Treating one exchange's liquidation feed as market-wide truth.

pass_fail_design_criteria_for_future_pre.fail: Forward returns are mixed, entirely symbol/month dependent, or similar to timestamp-block null events.
pass_fail_design_criteria_for_future_pre.pass: Across chronological segments, post-event forward returns are directionally positive after costs are ignored for event study, event frequency is sufficient, and effect is not dominated by one symbol.

possible_failure_modes: Liquidation cascade is trend continuation rather than exhaustion.
OI drop reflects liquidity withdrawal, not completed forced selling.
Funding data is too sparse or exchange-specific to identify crowding.
Recent free-history window is too short for robust tail-event statistics.
hypothesis_designs.why_others_cant_arb: The setup depends on forced deleveraging, latency of crowd unwind, and uncertainty about whether liquidations are complete; many participants cannot add risk during cascades.

anti_overfit_requirements: No parameter grid in the first event study.
Use round structural thresholds only, such as top decile event buckets or fixed multiples of recent median liquidation volume.
Chronologically split recent data if enough history exists; do not use later segments to tune earlier definitions.
Compare event windows to simple time-block nulls before any backtest is considered.
No candidate, edge, live, or capital permission from event-study results.
hypothesis_designs.expected_direction: Downward mean reversion after short-squeeze exhaustion and OI flush.
hypothesis_designs.mechanism: After a strong pump, a large short-liquidation spike with falling open interest can mark forced buying exhaustion after a short squeeze.
hypothesis_designs.minimum_history_needed: At least 30 recent 15m days for initial event study; 60 or more days preferred.
no_trade_conditions: Short-liquidation spike occurs while OI continues to expand.
Funding remains negative enough to suggest shorts are still crowded rather than flushed.
BTC and ETH are in broad market pump conditions.
The event is caused by listing or data discontinuity.
overfitting_risks: Picking pump-return and liquidation thresholds from best examples.
Selecting only symbols with visible squeeze reversals.
Using holdout-like recent days to tune event definitions.

pass_fail_design_criteria_for_future_pre.fail: Events continue upward, are too rare, or depend on a single pump episode.
pass_fail_design_criteria_for_future_pre.pass: Forward returns after short-liquidation/OI-flush events are negative across multiple symbols and segments, with timestamp-block nulls less competitive.

possible_failure_modes: Short liquidation spike starts a momentum breakout instead of ending one.
OI drop is too small to prove deleveraging.
Crowding proxies disagree across funding and long/short ratio.
hypothesis_designs.why_others_cant_arb: Short squeezes are reflexive and balance-sheet constrained; arbitrageurs may wait until forced buying slows, making exhaustion timing hard but potentially observable.

anti_overfit_requirements: No parameter grid in the first event study.
Use round structural thresholds only, such as top decile event buckets or fixed multiples of recent median liquidation volume.
Chronologically split recent data if enough history exists; do not use later segments to tune earlier definitions.
Compare event windows to simple time-block nulls before any backtest is considered.
No candidate, edge, live, or capital permission from event-study results.
hypothesis_designs.expected_direction: Continuation when OI builds without adverse liquidation; reversal when buildup is followed by liquidation shock against the crowded side.
hypothesis_designs.mechanism: Open interest rising with price expansion indicates leverage buildup; continuation may follow if no liquidation occurs, while liquidation against the crowded side may turn the move into a trap.
hypothesis_designs.minimum_history_needed: At least 60 days of 15m or 1h data for buildup periods plus event follow-through windows.
no_trade_conditions: OI and price move are directionally inconsistent.
Liquidation data is missing or side cannot be separated.
Funding is neutral and OI buildup is small relative to recent baseline.
overfitting_risks: Separating continuation/trap regimes after seeing PnL-like outcomes.
Choosing different event windows by symbol.

pass_fail_design_criteria_for_future_pre.fail: Field coverage is inconsistent, side labels are ambiguous, or event counts are too sparse.
pass_fail_design_criteria_for_future_pre.pass: Dataset review confirms stable OI trend, liquidation-side, funding, and price fields across the probe universe before any event outcome test.

possible_failure_modes: OI buildup reflects hedging, not directional leverage.
Liquidation shock arrives after the measured forward window.
Continuation and trap regimes cancel in aggregate.
hypothesis_designs.why_others_cant_arb: The signal separates healthy leverage expansion from forced unwind only after combining OI trend, price trend, and liquidation side; single-variable arbitrage is noisy.

anti_overfit_requirements: No parameter grid in the first event study.
Use round structural thresholds only, such as top decile event buckets or fixed multiples of recent median liquidation volume.
Chronologically split recent data if enough history exists; do not use later segments to tune earlier definitions.
Compare event windows to simple time-block nulls before any backtest is considered.
No candidate, edge, live, or capital permission from event-study results.
hypothesis_designs.expected_direction: Reversal away from the crowded funding side after OI contracts and liquidation confirms unwind.
hypothesis_designs.mechanism: Extreme positive funding suggests crowded longs and extreme negative funding suggests crowded shorts; OI contraction plus liquidation confirms crowd unwind.
hypothesis_designs.minimum_history_needed: At least 60 recent days because funding regimes are slower than 15m liquidation shocks.
no_trade_conditions: Funding is extreme but OI is still expanding.
Funding field is stale, missing, or exchange timing cannot be aligned.
overfitting_risks: Choosing funding cutoffs from best reversals.
Combining many confirmation filters until sample is tiny.
Using the same recent window for threshold discovery and evaluation.

pass_fail_design_criteria_for_future_pre.fail: Funding extremes alone dominate without confirmation, or confirmed events are too rare to evaluate honestly.
pass_fail_design_criteria_for_future_pre.pass: Extreme-funding plus OI/liquidation-confirmed events show consistent reversal direction across chronological segments.

possible_failure_modes: Funding remains extreme during persistent trend.
Funding is arbitraged by basis trades and not a clean directional crowding measure.
Free recent data has too few full funding cycles.
hypothesis_designs.why_others_cant_arb: Funding crowding can persist longer than expected, so naive contrarian trades are dangerous; forced-liquidation confirmation may identify the narrower unwind phase.

anti_overfit_requirements: No parameter grid in the first event study.
Use round structural thresholds only, such as top decile event buckets or fixed multiples of recent median liquidation volume.
Chronologically split recent data if enough history exists; do not use later segments to tune earlier definitions.
Compare event windows to simple time-block nulls before any backtest is considered.
No candidate, edge, live, or capital permission from event-study results.
hypothesis_designs.expected_direction: Short-term reversal opposite the liquidation side once OI confirms a flush.
hypothesis_designs.mechanism: A one-sided liquidation imbalance plus falling OI may distinguish forced exit from ordinary volatility and identify short-term exhaustion.
hypothesis_designs.minimum_history_needed: At least 30 recent 15m days for first event study; more history required before any backtest.
no_trade_conditions: Long and short liquidations are balanced.
OI does not fall during the liquidation event.
Price movement is small relative to recent volatility.
Event overlaps known data outage or incomplete exchange coverage.
overfitting_risks: Choosing forward windows after looking at event returns.
Dropping symbols with adverse liquidation behavior.

pass_fail_design_criteria_for_future_pre.fail: Imbalance events behave like normal volatility or are not distinct from random high-volume intervals.
pass_fail_design_criteria_for_future_pre.pass: One-sided liquidation plus OI-flush events show reversal behavior across sides, symbols, and chronological segments, with no single cascade dominating.

possible_failure_modes: Liquidation imbalance is a momentum confirmation, not exhaustion.
OI flush follows rather than precedes the tradable reversal window.
Event study is dominated by one market-wide cascade.
hypothesis_designs.why_others_cant_arb: The opportunity depends on transient forced flow and balance-sheet stress; crowd exits are not easily arbitraged while liquidation engines are still active.

limitations: This design uses only V5 availability metadata; it does not call Coinalyze or acquire new rows.
Coinalyze free data is recent and short-history, so any later backtest would require separate lockbox or paper-forward treatment.
Hypotheses are mechanisms for future testing, not candidates or edge claims.

recommended_hypothesis_priority.rationale: Most directly tests forced-flow exhaustion using liquidation side plus OI contraction and can be studied symmetrically for long and short cascades.
Clean forced-selling exhaustion mechanism after sell cascades.
Clean forced-buying exhaustion mechanism after short squeezes.
Useful crowding overlay, but funding can persist and should require OI/liquidation confirmation.
Broader regime concept that should start with dataset review because continuation/trap definitions are easier to overfit.
